risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization to the three treatment groups was stratified by participant’s baseline chronic pain grade and recruitment source ... Block sizes were equal to 3 or 6, and were chosen randomly with 2/3 and 1/3 probability, respectively. ... The randomization list was prepared using the “sample” function of the S-PLUS® statistical software" Comment: The sequence was generated using a computer-based randomization procedure in statistical software, with randomly chosen block sizes, which is consistent with a truly random allocation sequence.

Quote: "Treatment assignments were recorded on cards numbered consecutively within each stratum, and a study assistant not involved in the screening and randomization put the randomization assignments in sealed envelopes sequentially numbered by stratum. Randomization assignments were concealed to all study personnel with study participant contact until envelopes were opened by research staff at the time of randomization." Comment: This explicitly describes sequentially numbered sealed envelopes and states assignments were concealed from staff with participant contact until the time of randomization, which is sufficient to prevent foreknowledge of upcoming allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Potential participants were told that the study involved randomization to COCT or to one of two different versions of SMT. At no time before or during study participation were participants informed that one and only one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes." Comment: Participants and personnel could not have been fully blinded to the assigned intervention because the study compared a medication regimen with two different self-management therapies, and the interventions were visibly/experientially different. The paper also notes that the TSMT group received cycle-timed content and the COCT group received oral contraceptive management, making knowledge of allocation likely. The main outcomes (pain intensity, activity interference, depression, helpfulness) are subjective and plausibly influenced by awareness of treatment assignment, so the dominant performance-bias risk is high.

Summary: The main outcomes were patient-reported questionnaires/self-ratings (e.g. Graded Chronic Pain Scale, SF McGill Pain Questionnaire, Beck Depression Inventory, treatment helpfulness ratings), so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor. The report states that participants were told they would be randomized and that they were not told that one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes, but it does not explicitly state that participants or outcome assessors were blinded to allocation, nor that outcome assessment blinding was maintained. Comment: Because the outcomes were subjective self-reports and the paper does not clearly report blinding of outcome assessment, detection bias cannot be ruled out, but there is insufficient information to judge High risk confidently.

Quote: "In each of the three groups, 86% of participants completed at least one follow-up assessment. In the analysis sample, the three treatment groups did not differ significantly in rates of completion of both follow-up assessments (92% SMT, 98% TSMT, 92% COCT; P = 0.47)... We compared the analysis sample (n = 147) to study participants excluded from analysis (i.e., no follow-up data; n = 24)... There were no significant (P < 0.05) differences in treatment group assignment; recruitment source; age; education; marital status; race; the three baseline GCPS measures... or duration of facial pain." Comment: Attrition was relatively modest overall, follow-up completion was balanced across groups, and there was no evidence that those lost differed importantly at baseline. The authors also used an intention-to-treat approach with multiple imputation sensitivity analyses, making bias from missing outcome data unlikely.
